text: The transected proximal descending colon is visible, prepared for end colostomy formation during a laparoscopic abdominoperineal resection for low rectal cancer.
The specimen, likely the rectal cancer tissue, is prepared for extraction, positioned below the surgical field.
The surgeon uses fluorescence angiography with indocyanine green (ICG) injected systemically, employing a near-infrared enabled laparoscope to assess the perfusion sufficiency of the colostomy site.
The laparoscopic view shows the ankylostomy with insufficient fluorescence one minute after systemic injection of ICG, indicating potential perfusion issues.
The surgeon observes a small segment of mesocolon fluorescing brightly under near-infrared imaging, while the rest of the stoma remains dark, indicating insufficient perfusion.
Upon closer examination, the proximal segment of the colon exhibits fluorescence, indicating better perfusion and suitability for stoma formation.
The surgeon prepares to excise the poorly perfused segment of the colostomy, as identified by fluorescence angiography, before proceeding with stoma fashioning.
Under white light, the surgeon observes the surgical field, but the perfusion discrepancy visible in the near-infrared examination is not apparent.
